did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning algorithms, specifically the Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) with ResNet34, DenseNet121, and MobileNet-v2 backbones for skin cancer segmentation, and DenseNet121 for classification, on the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a skin cancer segmentation model using Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) with ResNet34, DenseNet121, and MobileNet-v2 backbones, and a classification model using DenseNet121 on the HAM10000 dataset. It reports specific results (e.g., 80% test accuracy) and is published in the proceedings of the 2023 2nd International Conference on Advancements in Electrical, Electronics, Communication, Computing and Automation (ICAECA).

title: INTELLIGANT SEGMENTATION AND CLASSIFICATION FOR SKIN CANCER PREDICTION
journal: 2023 2nd International Conference on Advancements in Electrical, Electronics, Communication, Computing and Automation (ICAECA)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of early skin cancer detection by proposing an automated computer-aided diagnostic system using deep learning. The authors implemented a segmentation model utilizing the Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) with ResNet34, DenseNet121, and MobileNet-v2 backbones, alongside a classification model using DenseNet121. The research was conducted on the HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,000 dermoscopic images of skin lesions across seven categories. Evaluation metrics included Intersection over Union (IoU) scores and loss values for segmentation, and accuracy for classification. The proposed methodology achieved a classification accuracy of 80% on the test set and segmentation accuracies of above 80%, 70%, and 75% for the respective backbones. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of FPN and DenseNet121 for skin cancer segmentation and classification tasks.

all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a skin cancer segmentation model using Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) with ResNet34, DenseNet121, and MobileNet-v2 backbones to perform semantic segmentation of skin lesions. Evaluation metrics included Intersection over Union (IoU) scores and loss values. The study reports segmentation accuracies of above 80%, 70%, and 75% for the respective backbones.

The paper proposes a classification model using DenseNet121 to classify skin lesions into seven categories using the HAM10000 dataset. The model achieved 80% accuracy on the test set. The task involves assigning input images to discrete diagnostic categories.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset of dermoscopic images of skin lesions, available on Kaggle. It contains images acquired from various populations using a variety of dermoscopic devices.

use.training: Used for training the DenseNet121 classification model (80% of data) and for segmentation tasks using FPN with ResNet34, DenseNet121, and MobileNet-v2 backbones.
use.validation: Used for validation of the DenseNet121 classification model (10% of data).
use.testing: Used for testing the DenseNet121 classification model (10% of data) and for evaluating segmentation performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, loss, and Intersection over Union (IoU) scores.

segmentation.notes: Segmentation was performed using Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) with ResNet34, DenseNet121, and MobileNetV2 backbones. Accuracy values are reported as greater than thresholds.

classification.notes: Classification was performed using DenseNet121. The model was trained for 15 epochs with a batch size of 32 and a learning rate of 0.001. Data split: 80% training, 10% validation, 10% testing.

architecture_used.main_model: Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) with ResNet34, DenseNet121, and MobileNet-v2 backbones for segmentation; DenseNet121 for classification.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained convolutional neural networks (CNNs) used as backbones (ResNet34, DenseNet121, MobileNetV2).

architecture_details.fpn_structure: Consists of a bottom-up pathway (typical CNN for feature extraction) and a top-down pathway (collection of up-sampling and lateral connections for feature fusing across scales). Lateral connections use 1x1 convolutional layers to adjust channel counts.
architecture_details.resnet34_details: 34 layers (33 convolutional, 1 fully connected). Uses skip connections to resolve vanishing gradient issues.
architecture_details.densenet121_details: 121 layers. Uses dense blocks with dense connections (concatenating output of preceding layer with following layer) for improved gradient flow and feature reuse. Transition layers composed of 1x1 convolution followed by average pooling or max pooling.
architecture_details.mobilenetv2_details: 53 layers. Uses depth-wise separable convolutions (depth-wise convolution followed by pointwise convolution).

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly reported (standard for Keras/TensorFlow DenseNet implementations, but not stated in text)
training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported for classification; Loss values reported for segmentation (Table I) but specific function name not stated.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) with ResNet34 backbone
Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) with DenseNet121 backbone
Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) with MobileNet-v2 backbone

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Listed as an augmentation technique in the image pre-processing steps.

conclusions: The proposed methodology using Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) with ResNet34, DenseNet121, and MobileNet-v2 backbones achieved segmentation accuracies of above 80%, 70%, and 75% respectively.
The DenseNet121 classification model achieved 80% accuracy on the test set of the HAM10000 dataset.
The study demonstrates the effectiveness of the FPN architecture for skin cancer segmentation and DenseNet121 for skin cancer classification.
The model's performance may vary depending on the specific dataset, preprocessing methods, and hyperparameters employed, indicating a need for careful evaluation and optimization for generalization.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which, while diverse, may not fully represent global skin cancer demographics, limiting external validation and generalizability.

Segmentation performance is reported using low IoU scores (0.24–0.39) and high loss values, with accuracy reported only as vague thresholds ('above 80%', '70%', '75%'), making precise performance assessment difficult.

The classification accuracy of 80% on the test set is moderate for a multi-class problem with seven imbalanced classes, suggesting potential limitations in handling class imbalance or complex lesion features.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals are reported to support the significance of the results.

The paper does not report per-class classification metrics (e.g., precision, recall, F1-score per class), which are critical for evaluating performance on rare or clinically significant classes like melanoma.

Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; while an 80/10/10 split is used, the paper does not detail if patient-level separation was enforced, which is crucial for HAM10000 to avoid overoptimistic results from duplicate images.
The study is published in a conference proceeding (ICAECA) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may indicate less rigorous peer review compared to top-tier medical AI journals.

Deployment concerns are not addressed; the paper does not discuss computational efficiency, inference time, or integration into clinical workflows despite mentioning MobileNet-v2 for potential efficiency.
